TEimEZVjN9o.title: SpaceX: The Most Tragic IPO In Stock Market History

buy_recommendations.reason: The creator is buying VGT instead of SpaceX stock and selling shares of the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ). VGT is Vanguard's information technology ETF which consistently outperforms the triple Qs, has lower management fees, and tracks an index that will not include SpaceX. This allows exposure to technology sector growth without the valuation risks and governance concerns of SpaceX.

stocks_to_avoid.reason: SpaceX is priced at 94 times revenue, not earnings, which is almost 50% more expensive than Palantir (the highest price-to-sales ratio in the S&P 500 at 65x), while growing almost two times slower and with much lower margins. The company is not profitable. The valuation grew 12 times faster than revenue growth. 93% of SpaceX's total addressable market is AI (primarily the troubled xAI acquisition), where the company has minimal traction and must compete against entrenched players like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. The core space business (Falcon 9, Starship) is operating at a $657 million loss despite being 24 years old. Starship development lacks regulatory approval, passenger transport regulations, and has no timeline for commercial viability. The company also has severe governance issues: Elon Musk controls 85% of votes with only 42% of shares, has been granted $175 billion in performance-based shares that he can vote and use as collateral before vesting with no requirement to actually build a Mars colony, and has a corporate opportunity carve-out allowing him to take business opportunities elsewhere. Shareholders waive jury trial rights and have no real governance power.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: Significant downside risk given the 94x sales valuation, unprofitability, slowing revenue growth relative to valuation expansion, governance control concentrated in a single individual with conflicting incentives, and forced inclusion in the Nasdaq 100 index at only 4-5% free float, which will artificially inflate the stock price as passive investors are forced to buy.

stocks_to_avoid.reason: The creator is selling shares of the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) because SpaceX will be added to this index just 15 trading days after its IPO under Nasdaq's new fast entry rule. This forces massive passive index funds to buy SpaceX stock at an inflated price despite the company's questionable fundamentals and governance. The stock has an extremely low free float of only 4-5%, meaning insiders controlling 95%+ of shares can effectively manipulate the price. Insiders can sell into a market where index funds are forced to buy, creating an artificial price inflation that will eventually unwind.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: The forced buying from index funds will artificially inflate SpaceX's stock price in the short term, creating valuation bubbles within QQQ that will eventually correct downward when the speculative demand subsides and reality of SpaceX's fundamentals becomes apparent.

key_warnings: Nasdaq index rule changes have eliminated protective mechanisms for retail investors. The elimination of the 10% minimum free float requirement and introduction of a 'fast entry' rule allowing companies into the Nasdaq 100 just 15 trading days after IPO removes critical safeguards that previously protected investors from overpriced IPOs and price manipulation by insiders with low free float.

SpaceX IPO represents a massive wealth transfer from retail investors to insiders. The combination of extreme overvaluation (94x sales), concentrated insider voting control (Elon Musk controls 85% of votes), modified lockup periods allowing insiders to sell 20% of eligible shares immediately after first earnings report, and forced buying pressure from index funds creates conditions for insider enrichment at the expense of public shareholders.

xAI/Grok acquisition is a liability masquerading as a growth asset. xAI represents 93% of SpaceX's total addressable market but has minimal enterprise traction (2 out of 1,000 users), faced a major scandal producing 4+ million explicit deepfake images including minors in an 11-day period, is facing multiple regulatory investigations and lawsuits, and Elon Musk himself stated the AI needs to be rebuilt from scratch. All 11 co-founders except Musk have left the company.

SpaceX's core space business is financially unviable at current operating metrics. The rocket business (Falcon 9, Starship) generated $4.1 billion in revenue but spent $4.8 billion operating it, resulting in a $657 million operating loss despite being 24 years old. SpaceX spends 60% more capital expenditure on xAI data centers than the entire space business combined, and Starship accounts for only 14% of total CapEx despite being critical to the company's valuation narrative.

CLLNEr3d8CM.title: Top Stocks I'm Buying For Huge Growth In June 2026

buy_recommendations.reason: Qualcomm just struck a deal to supply custom AI data center chips to ByteDance, gaining access to the largest AI market on Earth where Nvidia is locked out due to export controls. The custom ASIC chips are not on restricted export controls lists, making them production-ready through 2026 and 2027. Qualcomm becomes embedded in ByteDance's chip design and production process, making them harder to replace over time. Beyond ByteDance, Qualcomm's Orion CPU directly competes with Nvidia's Vera chip, and their AI 200 and AI 250 inference accelerators enter the market this year and next year. The stock has doubled over the last two months as the market reprices Qualcomm from a smartphone chip company to a competitor on two major fronts: data centers and edge computing. Qualcomm's automotive segment brought in $1.3 billion in revenues last quarter with 38% year-over-year growth, outpacing Nvidia's 6% automotive growth.

Arm just announced their AGI CPU, the first production chip Arm has ever designed, manufactured, and branded for themselves. This is a tactical entry into the data center CPU market after 35 years of collecting royalties from both sides of the chip war. The AGI CPU is more powerful than Nvidia's Vera CPU, requiring data centers to use around 40% fewer of them to support the same amount of GPUs. It has roughly double the performance per watt compared to Intel and AMD's chips. Arm expects to sell over $1 billion worth of AGI CPUs in the first year alone and hit $15 billion in annual revenue within five years, which would effectively quadruple the company's total revenues from the current $5 billion per year by 2031. This positions Arm to directly compete in Nvidia's market for the first time.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Arm's exact speed advantages depend on the actual workload, and there are plenty of cases where Nvidia still wins by large margins. Nvidia's CUDA platform has two decades of software, developer tools, and infrastructure that every AI team already relies on.

buy_recommendations.reason: Cerebras just completed the largest US semiconductor IPO ever and is directly competing with Nvidia in the data center AI accelerator market. Their wafer scale engine (WSE 3) is a fundamentally different architecture: instead of cutting wafers into individual chips, they turn the entire wafer into one massive chip with 62 times the size of competitors' chips, 19 times more transistors, and 44 times more AI cores compared to Nvidia's Blackwell. While having a quarter of the memory, the WSE 3 has 2600 times the memory bandwidth, allowing it to run Meta's Llama 4 Maverick model roughly 2.4 times faster than Nvidia's B200 because data moves across one giant chip instead of between multiple separate chips across cables and network switches. Cerebras is pairing this chip with Arm's AGI CPU, positioning them at the intersection of the major architectural innovations challenging Nvidia's dominance.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Cerebras's exact speed advantages depend on the actual workload, and there are plenty of cases where Nvidia still wins by large margins. Nvidia's CUDA platform has two decades of software, developer tools, and infrastructure that every AI team already relies on.

buy_recommendations.reason: Micron is the only US company making high-bandwidth memory for AI data centers, with competitors SK Hynix and Samsung based in South Korea and therefore more affected by tariffs, trade wars, and geopolitical conflicts. Micron's high-bandwidth memory is found in Nvidia's Hopper, Blackwell, and Rubin chips, AMD's Instinct MI300 and 400 series accelerators, and Google's TPUs. The company was already sold out of high-bandwidth memory for all of 2026 as of their earnings call. Micron reported record revenues of almost $24 billion last quarter, up nearly 200% year-over-year, with gross margins of 75% (better than most software companies), net income growing by almost 20x, and earnings per share growing by almost 30x. For next quarter guidance: 40% revenue growth, another 6% increase in gross margins, and 57% earnings growth quarter-over-quarter. Micron will make more money next quarter than any full year before 2025. The stock has skyrocketed 10x over the last year and tripled in price over the last 5 months but still trades at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of just 12, while other chip companies trade at 20 to 80 forward PE despite Micron's faster earnings growth.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Memory was historically a commodity, but Micron is now a core component of the AI revolution with exceptional growth.

buy_recommendations.reason: Rocket Lab is the only vertically integrated end-to-end space company that's publicly traded until SpaceX IPOs. While most space companies either build satellites or launch vehicles, Rocket Lab does all three. The Electron rocket is the most frequently launched small rocket in the world with a 3D-printed Rutherford engine (first rocket engine with 3D-printed combustion chamber, injectors, pumps, and propellant valves) that cuts manufacturing time from months to days and dramatically reduces cost per engine. Earlier this month Rocket Lab reported their best quarter ever with $200 million in revenue, up over 60% year-over-year, and a backlog that hit $2.2 billion, up 20% quarter-over-quarter and 100% year-over-year. The backlog has three buckets: small satellite launches on Electron and medium lift on Neutron, defense contracts for hypersonic test flights, and satellite/spacecraft manufacturing (2/3 of revenue). SpaceX is expected to IPO at roughly 60x forward revenue while Rocket Lab trades at 20x, suggesting Rocket Lab is relatively cheap even at an $84 billion market cap if SpaceX's valuation is justified. SpaceX is one of the first customers for Nvidia's Vera CPU, connecting the space and AI chip war narratives.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: The creator plans to wait for SpaceX's IPO (expected June 12th) and compare all space stocks side by side before making a final decision.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Space is the toughest market to operate in. Just days before this video, Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket exploded during a ground test in Florida, badly damaging the launch pad, causing the entire space sector to sell off. Competition in space lives and dies by launches. SpaceX is roughly 30 times bigger than Rocket Lab by revenue and launches 8 times more rockets per year, though they currently serve different market segments (big vs. small payloads).

key_warnings: Nvidia's reporting change to lump gaming GPU sales into an 'edge computing' segment that represents less than 8% of revenues gives competitors permission to directly compare their strongest business units to Nvidia's weakest and claim market share advantages, potentially inflating their stock prices as a result. Mainstream media focuses on headlines without looking below the surface, so stock prices are about to change in ways that reflect this competitive reality shift.

Nvidia is facing competition on multiple fronts: Qualcomm and Arm are shipping data center CPUs that compete with Nvidia's Vera CPUs; custom AI chips from Google, Amazon, and Cerebras are cheaper and more efficient for specific inference workloads; and companies like Apple, ARM, AMD, and Intel are competing in edge computing markets. While Nvidia won't outright lose these markets, competitors can now directly compare themselves to Nvidia in Nvidia's weaker markets.

The space sector is highly volatile and subject to catastrophic setbacks. Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket exploded during a ground test, causing the entire space sector to sell off the next day. Competition in space lives and dies by launches, making it an exceptionally risky sector.

buy_recommendations.reason: The creator believes Microsoft is significantly undervalued despite a 34% decline from all-time highs and 25% decline over the past month. The underlying business fundamentals continue to grow extremely well with operating cash flow at a decade high of 53.5% margin and growing at 18% annually. AI revenue surpassed $37 billion (up 123% YoY) and Copilot has 20 million paid seats (up 250% YoY). The software/productivity business revenue is at an all-time high of $135 billion (growing 14% annually) and Azure grew 39% in the most recent quarter. Microsoft has a defensible position in software due to wide enterprise distribution and proprietary data, which are key moats in an AI world. The three main bear arguments (AI disruption, CapEx ROI concerns, and OpenAI reliance) are all overblown: AI is a tailwind not a disruption, the company's strong operating cash flow growth proves positive CapEx returns, and the non-OpenAI cloud business is growing 28% YoY independently. The creator's conservative DCF model projects a fair value of $460 with a stock price of $600 by 2029, implying 20% compounded annual returns, and this doesn't even assume the business returns to historical valuation multiples.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: The creator indicates extreme interest in buying but states they have limited cash currently after recent purchases of Amazon and Meta. However, they are 'right on the edge' of adding Microsoft and find the current valuation compelling.
buy_recommendations.target_price: $600 by 2029 based on conservative DCF; fair value estimated at $460
buy_recommendations.cautions: The creator notes that the free cash flow expansion they expect will take time as the capex cycle must eventually slow (though this is inevitable and no hyperscalers are currently planning to slow spending). The personal computing segment is declining and only contributes ~10% of profits, but this is immaterial to the overall thesis since cloud and software are the key growth drivers. Capex spending levels remain elevated in the near term, which will continue to suppress free cash flow metrics.

key_warnings: Hyperscaler capital expenditure cycle concerns: The entire hyperscaler sector is facing market skepticism about whether massive capex spending will generate adequate returns, especially as chip prices remain elevated. Micron's recent earnings report showing continued price increases is causing renewed selloffs across hyperscalers. However, the creator argues this is a temporary market sentiment issue rather than a fundamental problem, as all hyperscalers are capacity constrained and demand remains strong.

Chip price inflation impact: Rising memory and semiconductor prices mean hyperscaler dollar efficiency is declining. For example, $1 billion in capex that previously bought a billion chips now buys only half a billion chips. This is eroding expected returns on infrastructure investments across the sector.

Market rotation away from hyperscalers: The market has rotated into memory and chip stocks instead of hyperscalers, leaving companies like Microsoft underperforming despite strong underlying fundamentals. This sentiment shift is structural and may persist in the near term despite improving fundamentals.

buy_recommendations.reason: Amazon is directly exposed to chips, robotics, cloud computing, artificial intelligence, and advertising—functioning like an ETF across multiple high-quality industries. The market is not giving Amazon sufficient credit despite underlying fundamentals growing very well. Amazon has a massive chips business that could be worth $50 billion in annualized revenue if sold to third parties (currently at $20 billion run rate), making it potentially the third-largest chips company in the world, larger than AMD. The company's advertising business is growing over 20% annually with acceleration, third-party seller services growing 18% annually, subscriptions growing 15% annually, and AWS growing nearly 30% with $137 billion in trailing 12-month revenue. Despite recent news about exploring chip sales to third parties, the stock sold off 11% over the past month, creating a buying opportunity.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: Current price (implied to be opportune based on recent 11% selloff)
buy_recommendations.target_price: $309 per share (based on creator's DCF assuming 16% operating cash flow growth annually over 3 years and 20x operating cash flow multiple)
buy_recommendations.cautions: Creator's DCF is conservative relative to analyst expectations of 28% operating cash flow growth. The stock has not produced much returns over the past 5 years despite fundamental growth, indicating a prolonged wait for multiple expansion may be required.

buy_recommendations.reason: Meta is one of the highest-quality businesses in the world with one of the strongest moats and access to vast resources, producing over $100 billion in annual operating cash flow. Despite negative sentiment around employee morale (CTO reports it's at a 20-year low comparable to Cambridge Analytica period) and recent layoffs, the underlying fundamentals continue to accelerate with top-line revenue growing over 30%, advertising business firing on all cylinders, and operating cash flow reaching an all-time high and growing over 20% per year. The negative sentiment and risks are already priced into the stock, which trades at only 18x forward earnings and 11.8x operating cash flow—well below historical averages and S&P 500 multiples—despite Meta being the fastest-growing hyperscaler. From current depressed valuations, if sentiment improves, the stock will benefit from both multiple expansion and continued fundamental growth.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Significant employee morale issues with repeated layoffs since 2022 (most recent: 8,000 employees or 10% of workforce in May 2026). CTO acknowledged company did 'atrocious job' explaining vision and supporting employees. However, creator believes this is a solvable short-term headwind, not a permanent impairment to fundamentals.

buy_recommendations.reason: Mercado Libre is in a 40% correction from all-time highs while revenue has grown 8x since 2020 at a 49% compounded annual growth rate and is still growing 49% year-over-year despite $32 billion in annual revenue—the most consistent high-growth company in the market. The company has evolved from e-commerce to a comprehensive ecosystem including advertising (growing 65-73% annually, reaching $1.7 billion in trailing 12 months, gaining market share from 2% in 2020 to 10%), digital banking (83 million monthly active users compounding 31% annually), credit portfolio ($15 billion doubling annually), fintech acquiring ($204 billion growing 36% annually with 9% market share across Latin America), and top-three digital ads platform. Gross merchandise volume is growing faster than Latin America market, indicating consistent market share gains. New YouTube partnership in Mexico and Argentina allows direct purchases through Mercado Libre due to their most reliable and largest logistics network. Insiders are actively buying (recent $200k, $1M+ purchases), historically signaling belief stock is cheap. Mexico represents massive runway as only 54% have bank accounts and 85% use cash for small purchases.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: Current price (down 40% from highs); creator has been buying since 2024 at $1,800 per share
buy_recommendations.cautions: Stock price has been flat over past 5 years despite tremendous business growth, indicating prolonged disconnect between share price and fundamentals. Creator acknowledges losses from 2024 purchases at $1,800 when current price is $1,600, emphasizing this is a long-term investment that may take years for appreciation.

buy_recommendations.reason: Brookfield's business centers on owning physical assets that make up the backbone of the global economy—data centers, terminals, railroads, oil pipelines, natural gas facilities—which are becoming increasingly valuable in today's world. CEO stated this will be a record year for the company and projects 20% annual earnings growth through 2030 with decades of runway ahead. The company also offers a rare combination of over 4% dividend yield with projected 15% annual dividend growth, which is uncommon in the market. Infrastructure build-out globally provides significant tailwinds and more capital deployment opportunities.

Constellation owns over 1,000 vertical market software companies operating in small niche industries (typically $1-10 million revenue each) that are largely ignored by private equity and larger firms. The software is sticky because it represents less than 1% of customers' annual expenses, is mission-critical to operations, employees are trained on it, and replacing it is risky and not cost-effective. AGM discussion (4 hours) revealed customers are not leaving, not building their own software, and are actually going to Constellation to build new AI features, with organic growth accelerating. The company is a serial acquirer deploying record capital at higher returns due to software sector being out of favor (prices down, less competition). Stock has fallen over 50% from highs but still trades at only 15x free cash flow while growing over 20% annually. Management has zero dilution, zero stock-based compensation, and massive shareholder alignment. Creator believes Constellation will be a net beneficiary of AI (not disrupted), and the market will eventually differentiate between software companies being disrupted versus those not being disrupted.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Canadian stock not available on many platforms like Robinhood; requires Interactive Brokers or similar for access. AI sentiment has negatively impacted software stocks broadly, but creator believes this is temporary market confusion specific to certain software segments.

buy_recommendations.reason: Topicus is a smaller Constellation Software subsidiary focused on Europe. It operates under the same thesis, business playbook, and culture as the parent company, offering exposure to vertical market software with strong competitive moats at attractive valuations.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Smaller subsidiary with less liquidity than parent company.

buy_recommendations.reason: Signity is Constellation's smallest public subsidiary, operating in Poland and growing at 40% per year while trading at approximately 12x cash flows. It is hidden in the market due to its small size and Polish listing but is executing extremely well with growth significantly outpacing its valuation multiple.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Very small subsidiary with limited market awareness and liquidity; Polish listing makes it accessible only through international brokers.

key_warnings: Software sector has been broadly punished by market sentiment around AI disruption, but not all software companies face the same risk. Vertical market software (Constellation Software) is actually benefiting from AI as customers use their platforms to build new AI features rather than replacing them. Investors should differentiate between software being disrupted versus software enabling disruption.

Market sentiment and multiple compression can persist for extended periods even as fundamentals improve, as evidenced by Mercado Libre (flat 5-year returns despite 49% CAGR revenue growth) and Amazon (minimal returns past 5 years despite accelerating fundamentals). Long-term investors must be prepared for multi-year holding periods before share prices catch up to business growth.

cdpOpOntLeU.title: My Warning to SPCX Investors - Here's What You Need to Know

buy_recommendations.reason: Daniel Pronk chose to invest in Amazon as part of the infrastructure layer for artificial intelligence. He views Amazon as building the actual infrastructure for AI and functioning as a utility for AI rather than being a model company itself. This approach allows exposure to AI growth while avoiding the capital intensity and commoditization risks of AI model development.

stocks_to_avoid.reason: Daniel Pronk and Steve Eisman both argue that SpaceX is massively overvalued at a $2.5-3 trillion market cap based on speculative, momentum-driven hype rather than fundamentals. The business has become extremely capital intensive, with CapEx at 215% of revenue in Q1 (compared to 42% in fiscal 2023), largely due to AI/Grok investments that are not yet producing returns. The prospectus reads like science fiction with unrealistic projections (asteroid mining, space data centers) and the valuation assumes 50x revenue growth over 5 years to justify even conservative 20-30x sales multiples by 2030. The LLM business (Grok) appears to be commoditizing along with all other AI models, offering no differentiation or moats while consuming enormous capital. Steve Eisman expects Tesla could be merged into SpaceX, which would further dilute shareholder value since Tesla's business is also struggling (earnings declining for 4 years, EV margins compressing). The entire valuation is based on a $28.5 trillion TAM where 85% relates to AI, not actual space operations.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: Pronk explicitly states the stock is being held up by hype and air, with minimal fundamental basis. A major correction is likely when the 95% insider-locked shares begin unlocking starting August 11, releasing massive selling pressure over 6 months. Current share price movements are artificially inflated due to only 5% of float trading; with full float available, the stock could decline sharply as rational sellers enter the market.

stocks_to_avoid.reason: Daniel Pronk argues that Tesla is not a good business fundamentally. Earnings have been declining for the last 4 years, revenue has been flat for years, and the EV business is seeing declines in both gross profit and revenue. The core issue is that Tesla is being valued not as a car company (which is 95% of its actual business today) but as a highly profitable software, robotics, AI, and energy company combined. This means investors are already pricing in massive future product successes across multiple categories that may never materialize or may be far less profitable than expected. Steve Eisman emphasizes that the EV market is not a good business as China produces EVs much cheaper than Tesla. If SpaceX acquires Tesla (as Eisman expects), it would be a value-destructive move for SpaceX shareholders.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: If Tesla's future products (autonomous vehicles, energy, software) fail to deliver at the profitability levels currently priced in, the stock faces severe downside. Even if these products work, they may not be as profitable as current valuations assume, leading to significant multiple compression.

key_warnings: The entire tech sector, particularly AI-focused companies, is in a hype-driven momentum phase disconnected from fundamental valuations. Companies like Google, Meta, and Microsoft are raising massive amounts of capital for AI infrastructure despite already having strong cash flows, indicating the capital intensity of AI has become unsustainable and is transforming the sector from asset-light to asset-heavy. Pronk warns that LLMs are rapidly commoditizing with no differentiation or pricing power between ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, and others—despite hundreds of billions in CapEx spending, the products are increasingly interchangeable, suggesting future profitability is at risk.

There is a bifurcated market where speculative, hype-driven stocks like SpaceX are trading at extreme valuations (90+ times sales) while high-quality, fundamentally sound businesses are being ignored and trading below COVID crash valuations (e.g., Mastercard). This suggests significant long-term value exists outside the momentum names but investors are irrationally chasing hype.

SpaceX's IPO float mechanics create artificial price support that will evaporate: only 5% of shares are currently trading while 95% are insider-locked. Starting August 11 and continuing through December 9, massive share unlocks will release enormous selling pressure as long-term holders gain the ability to exit. This structural dynamic means the current price spike is not indicative of true demand and could reverse sharply when insiders can finally sell.

Retail investors are being lured into speculative positions by social media posts projecting unrealistic returns (e.g., $7,000 per share, $90-100 trillion market caps, 10x returns in 5 years) based on circular reasoning and science fiction scenarios. These projections assume 50x revenue growth in 5 years and ignore the capital intensity of the business. Pronk warns this is irrational market behavior, not rational investing, and recommends avoiding participation rather than shorting.

Vn-ggZboJQw.title: Revealing My New Largest Stock Position

buy_recommendations.reason: Tasmea is a diversified maintenance and repair services company operating across mining, electrical, mechanical, civil, and water sectors in Australia. The company has compounded revenue and pro forma EBIT by 34% annually since fiscal year 2021, with pro forma earnings compounding at 34% and statutory net profit after tax at 49%. The business model is based on recurring revenue from essential maintenance contracts that are not dependent on commodity cycles. Tasmea has recently acquired Maxim Group, a leading data center electrical contractor, which is immediately 31% earnings per share accretive and provides exposure to Australia's booming data center sector (projected to grow 21% annually to 2030 with a 9 gigawatt pipeline). The company also benefits from Australia's electrification revolution and battery storage expansion (33 gigawatt pipeline growing 62% year-over-year). Management alignment is exceptional: insiders own 60% of the company, have never sold a single share, and continue actively buying on the open market. The company trades at only 22 times trailing twelve-month earnings despite its 15% organic growth guidance and historical 30%+ annual growth, making it cheap relative to peers who trade at 20-25x forward earnings. The creator believes the stock remains undervalued despite being up over 100% and hitting all-time highs.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: The creator has been buying in the $7.50 to $8.00 range and continued buying after the Maxim acquisition announcement. Current price mentioned at $8.59.
buy_recommendations.cautions: The stock trades only on the Australian Stock Exchange and is only accessible through Interactive Brokers, making it inaccessible for investors using Robinhood, Questrade, or Wealthsimple. The stock has already doubled from the creator's initial purchases and is trading at all-time highs, which may make some investors uncomfortable with entry timing despite the creator's valuation argument.

key_warnings: The creator mentions that Tasmea's competitors trade at forward PE multiples above 20 and into 25, and acknowledges an argument could be made that competitors are expensive and overvalued. Investors should be cautious about the broader valuation environment for industrial services and infrastructure contractors in Australia.

Investors must model either: (1) revenue deceleration with margin expansion, or (2) continued 20-30% revenue growth through 2032 with suppressed margins. Company is still rolling out products across Latin America (e.g., credit card launched in Argentina August 2024 but not yet in Chile/Colombia), so execution risk remains. Stock experienced 7% decline on negative sentiment despite strong fundamentals.

key_warnings: Hyperscaler capex is reaching unsustainable levels relative to operating cash flow. Google, Meta, Amazon, and Microsoft all have capex guidance now exceeding or approaching their operating cash flow generation, requiring debt issuance and equity dilution to fund continued AI infrastructure buildout. By 2028, capex growth rates will likely decelerate significantly as operating cash flows are tapped out. Since hyperscaler capex functions as a 'capex stimulus package' for semiconductor, construction, and energy stocks, this deceleration will create a major headwind for AI-related equities and valuations by 2027-2028.

Market is experiencing 'shiny object syndrome' and FOMO similar to 2000 dot-com bubble. Capital is rotating out of high-quality, boring businesses (Amazon, Meta, Microsoft) into new hot themes (semiconductors, chips, energy), causing massive valuation disparity. High-quality businesses are trading at COVID crash valuations despite stronger fundamentals, while speculative AI/chip stocks trade at elevated multiples. This creates significant opportunity in overlooked quality names but represents a bubble risk in concentrated hot sectors.

Equity dilution cycle beginning for mega-cap tech. Google announced $80 billion equity raise at 20-year high valuations (25.2x operating cash flow vs 17.7x historical). Meta rumored to consider equity issuance despite trading at 12x OCF (half of Google). This represents a reversal from years of share buybacks and signals that hyperscalers are hitting financing constraints. Meta specifically should avoid equity issuance given cheap valuation; debt would be preferable with strong balance sheet and $81B cash vs $59B debt.

buy_recommendations.reason: Limbach is a specialty contractor providing critical maintenance services to mission-critical buildings (hospitals, data centers, industrial facilities, universities) across the Eastern United States. The company has undergone a successful transformation from low-margin GCR (new construction) revenue to high-margin ODR (recurring maintenance) revenue, with ODR growing 282% since Q1 2021 while GCR declined 46%. Management states this transformation is complete, positioning the company for accelerated total revenue growth. The data center business is newly ramping, already representing 27% of Q1 2026 bookings growth after just one quarter of focus, with a $30+ million contract won from a hyperscaler. The company reaffirmed full-year 2026 guidance for 13-17% revenue growth and 10-16% IBITA growth despite a weak Q1, indicating strength expected in H2 2026. The stock fell 37% on earnings despite meeting pre-guided weak Q1 results, creating a valuation disconnect. Limbach trades at 23x forward P/E and 14.3x free cash flow versus peers trading at 30-70x forward P/E, and below its own historical averages. The company projects ~$70 million free cash flow for 2026 (75% conversion from IBITA), and the data center opportunity is not yet priced in given the company's goal to become the single national contractor for hyperscaler data center portfolios.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: Following 37% post-earnings decline in May 2026; creator actively accumulating at depressed levels
buy_recommendations.cautions: Weak Q1 2026 results and near-term revenue lumpiness due to the 6-9 month booking cycle. Success depends on execution of data center projects and achieving guidance in H2 2026.

buy_recommendations.reason: MercadoLibre is the largest e-commerce platform in Latin America with 33% market share and growing faster than Amazon at over 30% year-over-year, continuing to take market share. E-commerce penetration in Latin America is only ~10% of total retail sales, indicating substantial runway for growth. The advertising business has accelerated to 73% year-over-year growth in Q1 2026, reaching $1.75 billion in trailing twelve-month revenue with ~80% operating margins. Ads revenue as a percentage of GMV (10%) remains well below Amazon's (6%) on a per-unit basis, providing significant upside potential. Ads business alone could be valued at $48 billion at a 30x multiple. The fintech business generated ~$14 billion in trailing twelve-month revenue, nearly equivalent to all of Nuvbank's $17.5 billion revenue, suggesting a standalone valuation of ~$50 billion. Combined, the ads and fintech businesses are worth more than the entire $82 billion market cap, not accounting for the core e-commerce business. At current valuation, the market is not pricing in the substantial growth potential or the margin expansion that will occur when the company eventually reduces investment intensity.

buy_recommendations.reason: Nuvbank is a digital bank operating across South America with Brazil as the largest profit engine, now expanding into the United States. The company is growing revenues and net income by over 40% year-over-year with a trailing twelve-month net income of 3.2 billion in Q1 2026, having reached all-time highs. Customer base hit 135 million with average revenue per active account at $16, while cost to serve has declined significantly—from $110/month in Q4 2020 to current levels—creating operating leverage. Monthly average revenue per customer grew from $3.30 to $16 while costs remained flat, generating substantial net income acceleration. Brazil is the leading digital bank with significant TAM remaining, while Mexico—with 15 million customers and $124 ARPU—is now break-even and should contribute positive net income going forward, representing an equally massive untapped TAM. The new CFO hire from Visa (former CFO of Visa North America, the company's largest business) is viewed as a bullish signal for scaling operations. Nuvbank trades at only 18.4x trailing P/E and 12.4x forward P/E despite 40%+ revenue growth, 30-40% net income growth, and massive remaining TAM across multiple geographies.

buy_recommendations.cautions: Recent CFO resignation caused stock decline. Currency and regulatory risks in operating across multiple Latin American countries and new U.S. expansion. Success depends on Mexico profitability ramp and U.S. market entry execution.

buy_recommendations.reason: Mastercard is a highest-quality business with strong structural moats in global payments. While facing concerns about government-backed internal payment systems (e.g., Brazil's Pix, India's UPI), these internal systems are largely limited to domestic bank-to-bank transfers and remain useless for international purchases, travel, credit building, buy-now-pay-later, and e-commerce. Credit cards retain value for cross-border transactions, travel, rewards, protection, and online purchases. Evidence: Nuvbank has issued hundreds of millions of Mastercards in Brazil despite Pix adoption, and credit card usage remains strong. Mastercard is pivoting beyond pure payment network to value-added services (data, cybersecurity) that now comprise over 40% of revenue and grow faster than the payment network itself, diversifying cash flow and reducing reliance on any single moat. The business continues to grow strongly despite payment system disruption concerns. Mastercard stock has sold off to decade-low price multiples—lower than during COVID and the 2022 sell-off—despite continued strong growth and margin expansion.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: Decade-low valuation multiples provide compelling entry
buy_recommendations.cautions: Concerns around potential disruption from government-backed payment rails and competition from alternative payment systems, though creator believes the moat remains intact for international and cross-border use cases.

buy_recommendations.reason: Meta announced Meta Business Agent, an AI-powered assistant for businesses of all sizes, with over 1 million businesses already using it on WhatsApp and Messenger. The platform is being rolled out globally for free initially, then monetized via tiered subscriptions for businesses of all sizes. The AI agent can answer questions, make product recommendations, book appointments, qualify leads, and handle customer service 24/7. Beyond sales, the agent provides business owners with intelligent briefings summarizing customer feedback, pain points, and trends from millions of customer interactions—essentially functioning as a digital employee. Meta can monetize this across its 3.5 billion daily active users and tens of millions of businesses using WhatsApp, Instagram, and Facebook. This represents a new recurring, high-margin revenue stream that diversifies away from advertising. Current growth is accelerating to over 30% year-over-year. A conservative DCF assuming only 15% annual operating cash flow growth (a deceleration from current ~30%) and 13x price-to-operating-cash-flow (well below the historical median of 17.4x) yields 16% CAGR and a fair value of $732 per share within three years. The stock is not priced for this optionality or the ability to monetize the massive user and business base.

buy_recommendations.target_price: $732 in DCF (3-year fair value under conservative assumptions); $1,000 potential stock price if metrics achieved
buy_recommendations.cautions: Monetization execution risk on business agents—success depends on adoption rates, willingness of businesses to pay subscription fees, and competitive responses. Regulatory risk in multiple jurisdictions. Cyclical advertising revenue remains a significant portion of the business.

key_warnings: The new Federal Reserve under Kevin Warsh has removed forward guidance and interest rate communication, creating increased volatility around FOMC events. With less transparency, markets will make wild assumptions about Fed policy, leading to more volatility. Additionally, the Fed is divided (only one official saw a cut, nine saw at least one hike with six seeing more than one hike), and the base case is at least one rate hike by December with no cuts in sight until next year.

Historically, July is the best performing month for stocks (average S&P 500 return of 2.53%, Nasdaq 3.83%), and the last 10 Julys have all been up months. Data shows that if you bought QQQ at the close of June 22 every year for 15 years, you would have a 93% positive return with a median return of 4.7%. However, after July through the midterms in October, expect a significant pullback. During midterm years, the market typically sees a slowdown from July to October with average drops of 17-19% as uncertainty peaks over elections, shifting policies, and changing approval ratings.

Big tech companies (Amazon, Microsoft) are spending massive amounts on CapEx with negative or declining free cash flow despite record revenues and profits. Amazon's free cash flow is currently negative despite all-time high gross profit margins and net income due to $44 billion spent on CapEx last quarter. Microsoft's free cash flow of $15 billion is paltry compared to previous quarters due to $31 billion in CapEx. The first signal to exit AI buildout stocks will be when one of these hyperscalers announces they are cutting back on CapEx spending.

AI buildout stocks carry higher volatility (higher beta) and cannot be evaluated using traditional metrics like PE ratio or price-to-sales. Investors must focus on backlog, revenue growth rate, expected earnings, and analyst estimates. Additionally, if a major hyperscaler misses earnings, it would break the narrative that these companies can spend whatever they want, potentially triggering a significant sell-off in the AI buildout sector.

pHH3ViUdYhM.title: 2 Stocks the Entire AI Market Depends On !!

buy_recommendations.reason: TSMC is the world's dominant contract chip maker and foundry that manufactures semiconductors for nearly every major fabless technology company including Nvidia, Apple, AMD, Broadcom, and Qualcomm. When it comes to the most advanced chips running AI training and inference at scale, TSMC holds something close to an absolute monopoly with its 3-nanometer and 2-nanometer process nodes being the cutting edge of what silicon can do today. AI has become the core of TSMC's business—the company's high-performance computing (AI accelerators, GPUs, and data center chips) increased 20% quarter over quarter to account for 61% of fourth quarter revenue. The company guided full-year 2026 revenue to grow above 30% in US dollar terms. TSMC is executing a coordinated multi-continent expansion of its 3 nanometer node with a new fab in Taiwan, a second Arizona fab, and Japan's second fab being upgraded to 3 nanometer. The CEO stated they are stepping up CapEx investments to increase N3 capacity, with the CFO noting that CapEx in the next three years will be significantly higher than the past three years (which totaled 101 billion), representing a capital investment cycle of a scale semiconductor manufacturing has never experienced. The shift from generative AI to agentic AI is driving a step up in token consumption and computation needs, with AI accelerator revenue now tracking towards a compounded annual growth rate in the high 50s, potentially nearly doubling in under two years.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Geographic concentration risk—Taiwan is where the overwhelming majority of TSMC's most advanced manufacturing happens, making any geopolitical escalations involving Taiwan an existential risk to the global AI hardware supply chain and the stock. The Q1 2026 call also surfaced a near-term supply chain vulnerability with situations in the Middle East creating uncertainty around specialty chemicals and gases essential to chip fabrication, though management said they don't anticipate near-term disruption but acknowledged it was too early to quantify any potential impact on profitability.

buy_recommendations.reason: ASML is a Dutch company that manufactures lithography systems, the machines that project circuit patterns onto silicon wafers to create semiconductors. Its extreme ultraviolet (EUV) machines are the only tools on Earth capable of printing today's most advanced chip designs, and no other company makes them. TSMC, Samsung, Intel, and every leading edge chip maker buy from ASML because there is no alternative. EUV is how 3 nanometer chips and 2 nanometer chips exist, and ASML's next generation high NA EUV platform is how the nodes beyond that will be printed. As chips become more complex, more EUV exposures are required per chip, and AI is accelerating this litho intensity even faster than any previous technology cycle. The CEO updated 2026 guidance, increasing the expected revenue range to between 36 billion and 40 billion euros while maintaining gross margin expectations of 51 to 53%. ASML is currently under-shipping demand and cannot build EUV machines fast enough to meet customer demand. By 2027, ASML aims to ship at least 80 tools (up from 44 in the prior year), effectively doubling the chip-making capacity it delivers through EUV alone. Additionally, AI-grade DRAM adoption is accelerating—DRAM now accounts for 51% of net sales systems (up from 30% in Q4 2025), representing a durable new dimension to ASML's growth driven by AI accelerators requiring high bandwidth memory. Once memory makers commit to EUV intensive nodes, they keep buying tools for years, making this a sticky revenue stream rather than a one-time event.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Primary risk is regulatory. ASML is already prohibited from selling its most advanced EUV chips to China, and the rules governing what it can sell and to whom are still evolving. China currently accounts for 20% of ASML's revenue, and any further tightening of restrictions or extensions of controls to additional product lines could trim that contribution further. Management acknowledged this directly, noting that 2026 revenue guidance range is set specifically to accommodate potential outcomes of ongoing discussions around export control. For a company with a hardware monopoly on the world's most critical technology, the risk isn't competition, it's policy, and the regulatory environment remains live and unpredictable.

key_warnings: Geopolitical risk concentrated in Taiwan: Taiwan is where the overwhelming majority of TSMC's most advanced manufacturing happens. Any geopolitical escalations involving Taiwan is an existential risk to the global AI hardware supply chain. This represents a critical systemic vulnerability for the entire AI infrastructure buildout.

Supply chain vulnerability in Middle East: The situations in the Middle East is creating uncertainty around specialty chemicals and gases essential to chip fabrication. While management does not anticipate near-term disruption, they acknowledged it was too early to quantify any potential impact on profitability for semiconductor manufacturers.

Regulatory risk for semiconductor equipment exports: Export control regulations governing what ASML can sell to whom remain evolving and unpredictable. China currently accounts for 20% of ASML's revenue, and further tightening of restrictions could significantly impact the company's financial performance.

dPagBzggPXU.title: This is What "Always" Happens Before a Financial Crisis

key_warnings: Watch for major innovations reshaping the economy where people cannot fully predict value or effects. Historical pattern shows innovations like trust companies (1907), investment trusts (1920s), and mortgage-backed securities (2008) preceded major financial crises. The danger is when investors, businesses, and institutions rely on untested innovations under stress conditions.

Monitor financial sector exposure to bubbles and new innovations. When banks, real estate, and lenders become dependent on a bubble asset, a market problem can turn into a global economic problem. The 1929 Great Depression and 2008 Great Recession both spread from financial sector leverage tied to bubbles, affecting the entire real economy.

Track debt and leverage levels throughout the financial system. Leverage amplifies gains during booms but amplifies losses during downturns. The 2008 crisis featured leverage ratios exceeding 30:1 at some banks. Rising debt levels combined with other warning signs (new innovation, financial sector exposure, weakening confidence) indicate elevated crisis risk.

Assess confidence in institutions and the financial system itself. Loss of confidence is typically a late-stage warning sign but indicates a crisis is already underway. When questions shift from 'Are these assets worth the price?' to 'Can I trust the banks and government managing the economy?', systemic risk has become acute.

Be aware that financial crises develop over years and markets can continue climbing long after warning signs appear. Do not attempt to time market crashes through day trading. Instead, maintain preparation and avoid panic, as patient investors who remained composed during past crises (Great Depression, 2008) experienced generational wealth opportunities as markets recovered.

eAqFkUSVF1E.title: I’m Up 1,200% on This AI Stock. Here’s What I’m Doing Now

buy_recommendations.reason: The creator maintains a position in Micron despite a 1,200% gain, believing the company is in a super cycle driven by AI demand. Micron is trading at a low forward PE multiple of around 10x with a non-existent PEG ratio and extremely low valuation metrics across EV/sales and price-to-free-cash-flow. Revenue is up 1,667% over the last 12 months with gross margins at 84.56% and operating margins at 80%, representing historic highs. The company has secured $100 billion in minimum backlog through multi-year strategic customer agreements (16 agreements representing 20% of DRAM and 33% of NAND volume through 2030), with $22 billion in deposit commitments including $10 billion coming in Q4. Management is executing well with capex expansion to $27 billion (raised from $25 billion) and a $40 billion capex forecast for fiscal 2027 to meet future demand. Cloud memory demand is up 307%, and the company beat Q3 consensus estimates significantly. The creator sees demand remaining strong for the next couple of years with the core thesis that the memory cycle will last longer and go higher than expected.
buy_recommendations.cautions: The creator emphasizes this is house money and any position should be sized accordingly given the 1,200% gain. The key risk is not revenue growth but margin compression—at current 80%+ operating margins, the creator believes Micron will never trade at a 30x PE and may face pressure as customers seek to optimize memory costs through hardware and software solutions. Pricing power is expected to decline once supply catches up to demand, which the creator estimates could occur around fiscal 2027 when capex growth cycles peak. When big tech companies signal slower capex growth (even if still positive), negative headlines could create sharp drawdowns despite underlying business strength. The creator warns that memory is a cyclical business, and despite being in a super cycle now, this cannot last indefinitely.

Memory chip industry faces structural margin pressure. While Micron currently enjoys historic 84% gross margins and 80% operating margins, the creator emphasizes that companies like AMD, Nvidia, and Google do not like paying premium prices for memory. Hardware and software optimization by customers will eventually compress Micron's pricing power and margins, even if revenue continues growing. This is the core risk to Micron's valuation.

AWS is experiencing reacceleration in growth and margin expansion, with major customers like Anthropic and OpenAI driving demand that will continue growing. Amazon is on track to generate over $1 trillion in revenue by fiscal 2028 (within 2 years), and the shopping business will become increasingly profitable through automation and AI. The creator emphasizes that AWS alone could be more valuable than many standalone chip makers. DCF valuation indicates Amazon should trade at $321 per share, representing 38.6% upside. The creator argues the market disrespects Amazon simply because it's already a massive company ($2.5-3 trillion), but this does not preclude further substantial appreciation.

All margin metrics have been improving over recent years. The company is maintaining 12-14% organic revenue growth guidance for 2026 and operating margin guidance of 31.5%. The major catalyst is advertising revenue, expected to reach $3 billion in 2026 (double 2025 levels), which represents high-margin dollars and is only in its second major year of expansion, with room for continued growth. The creator notes that sports and live content have become huge growth drivers with massive expansion potential. Netflix is also a global household name representing a premium asset. buy_recommendations.reason: The creator argues Uber is significantly undervalued despite being down 16.4% over 12 months and 28.8% from peak. The core thesis is that the market underestimates Uber's competitive moat and misunderstands its business scope. Waymo is valued at ~$13.3 billion in private markets, yet Uber trades at comparable valuation despite generating $53.6 billion in annual revenue, achieving 18.3% free cash flow margins, and being profitable—advantages Waymo does not possess. The creator dismisses the narrative that autonomous vehicles will be dominated by Tesla/Waymo (90%+ market share), arguing the market is large enough for multiple profitable players. Uber's current business is far from mature: mobility, delivery (huge contributor), commerce, and wholesale operations all have substantial runway. The company is positioned as a key distribution channel for autonomous vehicle fleets through its app ecosystem. Recent expansion into hotels (via Expedia partnership) demonstrates platform extension. Advertising and subscription businesses are growing rapidly. The company continues accelerating: 20.7% YoY growth last quarter, with expectations for a strong 2026 driven by World Cup tailwinds. US/Canada Uber operations are more lucrative than international, providing geographic upside. DCF valuation indicates fair value of $105 per share (47% upside from ~$71). kzUUsvQXhiQ.title: Robinhood's CFO Just Explained Why This Stock Is More Valuable Than You Think

buy_recommendations.reason: The CFO Sheila Verma explained that Robinhood is transitioning from a pure trading platform to a financial super app with three distinct growth arcs: (1) becoming the #1 platform for active traders through continuous product innovation, (2) increasing wallet share by offering banking, credit cards, robo-advisory, and other financial products, and (3) building a global financial ecosystem across retail, B2B, and institutional clients. The company achieved 60+ billion in net deposits in 2024 with 35% annualized growth, and maintained 20% net deposit growth even during Q1 2025's difficult macro environment (government shutdown, software sell-off, global conflict). The addressable market is massive: $20 trillion in US retail brokerage, $20 trillion in US retirement, and $7-10 trillion in US advisory, with Robinhood at only $350 billion in assets under management. The company is gaining market share in options (recently #1), gaining in equities (#2 and compounding), and has new high-growth products (banking already at $2 billion deposits with 100,000+ customers; credit card at $10+ billion annualized GMV with 800,000 customers and millions on waitlist; prediction markets as fastest-growing product ever). Banking is macro-agnostic and direct deposit penetration is at healthy 40%. The company maintains profitable growth (revenues growing faster than expenses), is vertically integrating by owning prediction market exchange (Rhombus JV with Susquehanna), and is deploying aggressive buybacks ($1.5 billion program authorized, $300M+ executed in Q1 2025 alone) while still investing in organic growth and M&A. International expansion is beginning with UK, EU, Canada, Indonesia, and Singapore markets, with early UK cohorts looking similar to early US cohorts suggesting 10+ year runway for international growth.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Execution risk is significant given the number of concurrent product launches and international expansions. Credit card delinquency and unit economics must be monitored carefully—the CFO emphasized deliberate and cautious rollout, comparing to Nubank and Upgrade (on pace) but noting Apple Card grew too fast and 'blew themselves up.' Banking and credit card products are still in early scaling phases and must achieve profitability at scale. Prediction markets pricing structure has been completely redesigned to be more competitive, which could impact near-term take rates. The company is dependent on continued net deposit growth and customer trust, which could be disrupted by market downturns or competitive threats. Regulatory risks exist especially internationally across multiple jurisdictions.

key_warnings: Equity market take rates are compressed and mature with thin spreads and high competition—Robinhood is intentionally targeting market share over take rate maximization, which means revenue growth from trading may decelerate despite volume growth. This is a long-term trade-off for ecosystem lock-in.

While the creator views this outcome as unlikely given Uber's 20+ global AV partnerships, it remains a tail risk that could eliminate Uber's entire business model if autonomous vehicles consolidate around one or two players.

1iOUIaSxvwo.title: One of My Favorite Stocks Could 10x According to Their Own Executive

buy_recommendations.reason: Executive describes Mercado Libre as capable of becoming a $1 trillion company (10x from current $86 billion market cap) based on massive underpenetrated markets in Latin America. Company has achieved 29 consecutive quarters of 30%+ revenue growth (49% in Q1 2026), with credit book growing 87%. The market is misunderstanding the size of the prize ahead: Latin America has only 15% of transactions online vs 85% offline, compared to Amazon's US consumer transacting 50 times per year vs Mercado Libre's 10-11 times. The company is intentionally compressing margins (from 15% to 7% EBIT) by lowering free shipping thresholds and issuing credit cards to unlock customer frequency and ecosystem stickiness. Three consecutive quarters show unit shipping costs falling 17% as volume scales, suggesting the model self-funds as it grows. By 2032, the executive expects Mercado Libre to be approximately 3x current size with a full suite of financial products, better customer personalization via AI, and continued exorbitant growth rates due to early-stage market penetration.
buy_recommendations.target_price: The executive mentions a potential $1 trillion valuation (implying 11-12x from $86 billion current market cap), though this is aspirational rather than a formal price target.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Company is currently in an investment cycle with margin compression, which some investors misinterpret as the 2017 logistics build-out (when EBIT went from 30% to -5%); however, current compression is only 15% to 7% and driven by controllable decisions (free shipping threshold reduction and credit card issuance). Growth is still constrained by physical infrastructure (logistics network capacity) and risk tolerance in fintech (credit book). Macro volatility in Latin America requires maintaining a healthy balance sheet and cash generation. Market may continue to undervalue the growth opportunity by modeling normalized growth rates (40%, 30%, 20% tapering down) rather than extended high-growth period.

key_warnings: Investors may be mispricing Mercado Libre by modeling normalized growth trajectories when the company itself expects continued 30%+ growth for another 5+ years due to massive underpenetration of e-commerce and fintech in Latin America. This represents a potential disconnect between sell-side models and management's view of the market opportunity.

Physical infrastructure constraints (logistics, warehouses, last-mile delivery in major cities) and fintech risk management are the binding constraints on Mercado Libre's growth speed, not market demand. This limits how aggressively the company can reduce friction without degrading service quality (NPS) and underwriting standards.

k4Y3UctGWuI.title: Top Stocks I'm Buying For Huge Growth In July 2026

buy_recommendations.reason: VGT serves as the foundation of the creator's portfolio. It holds over 300 companies, positioning it between the NASDAQ 100 and S&P 500 in diversification. The fund tracks the MSCI US Investable Market Information Technology Index, which focuses on US information technology stocks only. Over 80% of VGT's holdings focus on hardware, semiconductors, system software, and applications, while excluding non-tech companies like Walmart and Pepsi that dilute the NASDAQ 100. The fund has lower fees than SPY and QQQ while outperforming both indexes by large margins. Nvidia, Apple, and Microsoft comprise over 40% of the fund's weight, along with other quality companies like Broadcom, Micron, AMD, and Lam Research in the top 10.

Nvidia is at the very center of the entire AI revolution, making the chips that every AI model trains on and controlling the CUDA software ecosystem that no competitor can crack. The stock currently trades at a price-to-earnings ratio of 31, which is cheaper than any point in the entire AI revolution or the 3 years before ChatGPT launched. The global artificial intelligence market is expected to almost 19x in size over the next nine years, representing a compound annual growth rate of 38.5% through 2034. Nvidia's revenues have been accelerating, growing year-over-year by 55%, 62%, 73%, and 85% over the last four quarters. Nvidia will not be disrupted by another GPU maker since their hardware and software ecosystems are already deeply entrenched; it would take a fundamentally different kind of AI accelerator to chip away at their customer base one workload at a time.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Wall Street analysts keep predicting that Nvidia's growth will slow down, but the creator argues this narrative has been wrong every single quarter.

buy_recommendations.reason: Google owns its entire AI infrastructure stack: the chips (Tensor Processing Units), the data centers, the cloud, the Gemini models, and products and services used by billions of people worldwide. Google Cloud now generates over $80 billion in annual revenue with a backlog of almost half a trillion dollars, growing almost as fast as Amazon Web Services and Microsoft Azure combined. Google is the only company that runs more AI compute than any other company on Earth with their TPUs. Recently, Google started selling TPUs to outside customers, including a deal to sell up to a million chips to Anthropic, representing well over a gigawatt of new compute capacity coming online. The creator is so confident in Google's position that they note Google can arm their own rivals with their best chips and still win big due to their full-stack advantage.

Broadcom builds custom AI chips for everybody else, including Google, Meta, OpenAI, and Anthropic, making them one of Nvidia's biggest direct competitors. Broadcom's AI chip revenue hit $10.88 billion last quarter, up 143% year-over-year. The company is guiding for over $100 billion in AI chip revenue by the end of 2027. The creator's investment thesis is that regardless of whether the market shifts towards Nvidia's GPUs, Google's TPUs, or custom chips made by Broadcom, they win because they hold all three companies. This hedging strategy protects against uncertainty in which chip architecture dominates the AI infrastructure market.

TSMC builds over 90% of all advanced chips on Earth and around 70% of the world's chips by revenue. They are the only company that can manufacture advanced chips for every side of the chip war at scale, making GPUs for Nvidia and AMD, smartphone processors for Apple and Samsung, and custom AI chips for Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. TSMC is so far ahead of every other foundry that they can simply set the price and customers have to pay because there is nowhere else for them to go. Regardless of whether Nvidia's GPUs, Google's TPUs, or Broadcom's custom chips win in the market, TSMC gets paid either way, making them a defensive position in the portfolio.

AI chips are only as fast as the memory that supports them. High bandwidth memory is one of the biggest bottlenecks of the entire AI buildout, and only three companies on Earth make it, with Micron being the only one based in the United States. Micron's memory is already pre-sold through the end of 2027, and the memory shortage is expected to get worse before it gets better as AI demand increases. AI companies are signing multi-year contracts and prepaying for chips that aren't even built yet. Memory is no longer a cyclical commodity; Micron has significant pricing power for memory chips, and AI companies can either pay up or fall behind. The creator notes that TSMC and Micron are getting paid no matter which chip architecture wins.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Micron was about to report earnings at the time of recording; the creator invited subscribers to request a deep dive video after earnings are released.

buy_recommendations.reason: Every AI data center needs power and cooling. A single Nvidia rack today pulls around 120 kW (about 10 times more than traditional server racks), while Vera Rubin racks currently shipping take over 200 kW, with power requirements continuing to increase with each new generation. Nvidia partnered with Vertiv to redesign the entire power architecture from the ground up, moving data centers to 800 volts of DC power, the same standard Nvidia is building to eventually support 1 megawatt racks. Unlike most vendors who sell one piece of the puzzle, Vertiv sells both power and cooling as a single end-to-end architecture from the grid to the chip, all optimized to work together. Companies like Schneider, Eaton, and Delta are racing to the same 800V standard, but only Vertiv and Schneider deliver the full power and cooling stack as a single system. Vertiv also builds direct-to-chip liquid cooling. Every gigawatt-scale AI factory being built right now needs what Vertiv is selling, with orders growing by 250% year-over-year, the fastest growth in Vertiv's history.

Before anyone can order Vertiv systems, they need grid-connected power to run them. Iron has over 4.5 gigawatts of secured power across Texas, Oklahoma, Canada, and Spain, all being used for AI data centers. Nvidia itself signed a 5-year, $3.4 billion cloud contract to run internal workloads on Iron's infrastructure. As part of that deal, Nvidia took a warrant to buy $30 million shares of Iron stock for $70 per share, which only pays off if Iron trades well above that price over the next few years. Microsoft also signed a 5-year, $9.7 billion deal for Iron's capacity and prepaid for 20% upfront. Iron's $3.4 billion annual recurring revenue target only uses about 10% of the power they already have secured, meaning they can support 10 times that amount once all their AI infrastructure is constructed and running.
buy_recommendations.target_price: $70 per share (Nvidia's warrant strike price, implying 40% upside from the $49 price mentioned)
buy_recommendations.cautions: Building data centers costs significant money that Iron doesn't have yet, so they will need to keep raising capital to close that gap. The stock is volatile due to this capital intensity, which is a real risk. However, the creator views this as the same kind of spending being seen across the AI industry broadly.

buy_recommendations.reason: Meta currently trades at around 20 times earnings, the lowest multiple in the entire Magnificent 7, presenting a valuation opportunity. The company is spending between $125 and $145 billion in capex this year alone, close to double what they spent in 2025. However, their family of apps (Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp) has over 3.5 billion daily active users across the globe, so they need to spend aggressively on AI infrastructure to serve AI to almost half the people on the planet. While Wall Street analysts view Meta's spending as a risk, the company is already proving it's worth it: ad impressions are up 19% year-over-year, average price per ad is up 12%, and revenues are up 33% year-over-year. The creator employs a dollar-cost-averaging strategy as the stock drops.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: Dollar-cost averaging recommended; buy on dips
buy_recommendations.cautions: The stock is experiencing declines, which the creator views as a buying opportunity due to the low valuation multiple and strong underlying business fundamentals.

buy_recommendations.reason: Palantir has been on the creator's list for 3 years in a row. The company's revenue grew by 85% year-over-year, and they raised their full-year guidance to $7.6 billion, which would imply another 71% growth. Palantir also guided for $3.2 billion in US commercial revenue, up 120% year-over-year, making this a profitable pure-play AI software company that's almost doubling in size every single year. The global market for AI and software is expected to almost 7x in size over the next 7 years, representing a 32% compound annual growth rate from now through 2033, about twice the growth rate of the S&P 500 for 7 years straight. Palantier is positioned to take a meaningful slice of that growth with its AI software platforms. This exemplifies the creator's philosophy of getting rich without getting lucky.

Every dollar spent on AI infrastructure, models, software, or services is vulnerable to new kinds of cyber attacks, making cyber security not an option but the cost of doing business. CrowdStrike's Falcon platform has three integrated parts: a library of cloud-based modules for antivirus scans, firewall management, and malware protection; a proprietary threat graph that tracks connections between people, their devices, and networks they access (similar to Palantir's ontologies but specifically for enterprise networks); and the Falcon agent, a tiny piece of software running on every device that sends data back to CrowdStrike to determine when to run cloud modules and update the threat graph. The global cloud security market is expected to more than triple in size over the next 6 years, and this growth will accelerate further when accounting for AI-enabled cyber threats. CrowdStrike is well-positioned to capture significant growth in this expanding market.

key_warnings: Private equity and late-stage IPO capital routes: Companies like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic have valuations measured in the trillions of dollars but remain unprofitable today. These companies are eligible or expected to go public and enter the NASDAQ index, but their unprofitability represents a structural risk to the index. The creator specifically avoids the NASDAQ 100 due to this IPO rule change that allows stocks to enter after just 15 trading days post-IPO (versus 3 months to a year previously), exposing the index to overvalued unprofitable mega-cap companies.

AI infrastructure capital intensity risk: Companies building AI data centers (like Iron/Palantir) require massive ongoing capital expenditures that exceed current free cash flow. While the creator views this as justified by market opportunity, it creates execution risk and dilution risk as companies must continually raise capital to fund expansion.

Concentration risk in chip supply: Over 90% of advanced chip manufacturing is concentrated at TSMC, creating geopolitical and operational single-point-of-failure risk. Similarly, high-bandwidth memory production is concentrated among only three companies globally, with Micron being the sole US-based producer.

Market shift uncertainty in AI architectures: While the creator hedges against this by owning Nvidia, Google, and Broadcom, uncertainty remains about whether the market will prefer Nvidia's general-purpose GPUs, Google's custom TPUs, or Broadcom's custom chips, introducing competitive and revenue growth uncertainty across the AI chip infrastructure stack.

The Nasdaq rule change on May 1st eliminated the 3-month 'seasoning period' for new listings, allowing these companies to be added to the index immediately upon listing rather than waiting. This will force index funds tracking the Nasdaq 100 to execute large-scale, programmatic selling of existing positions to rebalance, particularly the mega-cap AI stocks (Nvidia at 13%, Alphabet at 11%, and the other large chipmakers and AI companies that make up 52% of the index). This forced selling will occur on a fixed schedule regardless of fundamentals, and could accelerate if continued inflows into the new IPOs cause them to take up even larger index weightings. The selling pressure is mechanical and will be difficult to avoid; however, the creator frames this as a buying opportunity for investors willing to deploy capital during the dislocation.

Newly listed IPO stocks are inherently volatile, often oversubscribed on the first day, and their initial prices rarely reflect true fundamentals. The real selling pressure typically arrives 90-180 days after IPO when lockup periods expire and insiders/early investors can sell. Adding $4 trillion in volatile, newly-public mega-cap companies to a market-cap-weighted index could amplify volatility during the rebalancing period.

While the creator presents the forced selling of large-cap tech stocks as a buying opportunity, there is a risk that the magnitude of the rebalancing could trigger broader market dislocations or sentiment shifts that affect valuations beyond simple technical rebalancing mechanics.

Meta is launching Instagram on TV with Reels casting and horizontal video content to directly compete with Netflix and YouTube for TV watch time. YouTube has captured substantially more market share growth than Netflix year-over-year (1.7% gain versus Netflix's 0.2% gain), suggesting competitors are outperforming.

Netflix's growth rates are consistently decelerating and the business is maturing. Analyst expectations show revenue growth declining from current levels to 13.75% (full year), 11.7% (2027), and 10.3% (2028), indicating a structural slowdown in the business rather than a temporary issue.

Market valuation is not solely determined by distance from all-time highs. A stock down 45% from peaks does not automatically represent a buying opportunity if the stock was overvalued at those peaks. Netflix's decline was primarily driven by multiple contraction from an elevated valuation, and the stock has normalized to fair value rather than becoming a discount.

Memory chip shortage narrative may be overblown by bearish sentiment. While Apple's attempt to source DRAM from Chinese supplier CXMT has generated fear, multiple structural hurdles could prevent meaningful market impact: (1) US Commerce Department must approve the deal amid inflation vs. strategic concerns, (2) Chinese government may block the export to protect domestic AI capacity and semiconductor independence, (3) Even if approved, Chinese suppliers cannot produce competitive HBM (high bandwidth memory), which is where the real AI-driven shortage exists due to 3-4x higher wafer consumption per gigabyte vs. standard DRAM. The shortage is structural until at least 2028 as new fab construction completes.

DRAM pricing power is real but concentrated in leading-edge and HBM production, not legacy nodes. Standard DRAM price relief from Chinese suppliers would address consumer electronics (phones, laptops, PCs) but not the bottleneck in AI infrastructure which drives the majority of current demand. Micron and Samsung are intentionally starving consumer memory to serve data center HBM demand, which is a structural choice, not a supply constraint that CXMT can relieve.

Political risk is extremely elevated and multifaceted: (1) Trump administration faces a no-win choice between allowing Apple to buy Chinese DRAM (cooling inflation but contradicting 5-year US chip strategy to contain China) or blocking it (protecting domestic/allied producers but allowing Apple price increases that worsen inflation), (2) Marco Rubio as Secretary of State adds complexity given his 2022 opposition to Apple-YMTC NAND deal, (3) China has demonstrated twice in 2025 it will reject American chip exports even when beneficial to block US AI advancement, and may do the same with CXMT exports.

However, investors should completely separate their AI buildout portfolio (high risk, volatile) from their conservative portfolio (mega cap stable stocks, healthcare, consumer staples) to properly manage risk. If an investor YOLOs AI stocks at the top using options, pullbacks to moving averages will shred their portfolio.

Memory supply constraints from Chinese memory companies buying to avoid Korean suppliers' high costs and supply constraints caused the recent China-related scare in the memory sector (DRAM ETF down 27% from peaks).
